title: Reproduction
summary: This lesson explores the intricate processes of human reproduction, from the formation of gametes (spermatogenesis and oogenesis) to the hormonal orchestration of the menstrual cycle. It covers fertilisation, implantation, the vital role of the placenta in foetal development, and the hormonal control of birth.
learningObjectives: Outline the processes of spermatogenesis and oogenesis, including the role of hormones.
Explain the hormonal control of the menstrual cycle, including the roles of FSH, LH, oestrogen, and progesterone.
Describe fertilisation, implantation, and the function of hCG in early pregnancy.
Explain the structure and function of the placenta and the hormonal control of birth.

simpleExplanation.title: The Blueprint of Life
simpleExplanation.summary: Human reproduction is a precisely timed sequence of events, governed by hormones. This journey transforms two single cells into a new individual, supported by a dedicated life-support system.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of human reproduction like a highly choreographed stage play. The script (DNA) is ready, but the performance requires precise timing and communication. Hormones act as the stage managers, sending cues to the actors (ovaries, uterus) to perform their roles at the exact right moment, from preparing the stage (uterus lining) to the grand finale (birth).
steps: First, specialised cells called gametes (sperm and eggs) are produced through meiosis, a process controlled by hormones from the pituitary gland.
In females, a monthly cycle prepares the body for pregnancy. Hormones cause an egg to mature and the uterus lining to thicken.
If fertilisation occurs, the resulting embryo travels to the uterus and implants in the wall. The embryo then releases its own hormone (hCG) to maintain the pregnancy.
A placenta develops to nourish the foetus. After approximately nine months, a hormonal positive feedback loop involving oxytocin triggers childbirth.

flashcards.back: The production of sperm in the seminiferous tubules of the testes. It is a continuous process starting at puberty, involving mitosis, cell growth, two meiotic divisions, and differentiation.
The production of eggs (ova) in the ovaries. The process begins before birth, pauses in prophase I, and completes only upon fertilisation. It results in one large ovum and smaller polar bodies.

flashcards.front: What is the role of FSH in males and females?
flashcards.back: In males, Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) stimulates Sertoli cells to support spermatogenesis. In females, FSH stimulates the growth and development of ovarian follicles.

flashcards.front: What is the role of LH in males and females?
flashcards.back: In males, Luteinizing Hormone (LH) stimulates Leydig cells to produce testosterone. In females, a surge in LH triggers ovulation and the development of the corpus luteum.
A temporary endocrine structure that forms from the remnant of the ovarian follicle after ovulation. It secretes progesterone and some oestrogen to maintain the uterine lining (endometrium).

flashcards.front: What is hCG and why is it important?
flashcards.back: Human Chorionic Gonadotropin. It is a hormone produced by the embryo after implantation. It maintains the corpus luteum, ensuring progesterone levels stay high to prevent menstruation. It is the hormone detected in pregnancy tests.

flashcards.front: Describe the function of the placenta.
flashcards.back: It facilitates the exchange of materials between mother and foetus (e.g., nutrients, oxygen, antibodies to foetus; CO₂, urea to mother) and acts as an endocrine organ, producing oestrogen and progesterone to maintain pregnancy.

flashcards.front: Explain the role of oxytocin in birth.
flashcards.back: Oxytocin, released from the posterior pituitary, stimulates contractions of the uterine muscle (myometrium). These contractions stimulate further oxytocin release, creating a positive feedback loop that intensifies until the baby is born.

The inner lining of the uterus. It thickens each month under the influence of oestrogen and progesterone to prepare for the implantation of an embryo. It is shed during menstruation if no pregnancy occurs.

flashcards.front: Distinguish between the functions of progesterone and oestrogen during the menstrual cycle.
flashcards.back: Oestrogen (from the follicle) stimulates the repair and growth of the endometrium and, at high levels, triggers the LH surge. Progesterone (from the corpus luteum) further develops and maintains the endometrium, making it receptive to implantation, and inhibits FSH and LH.

faq.q: Why does oogenesis produce only one egg while spermatogenesis produces millions of sperm?
faq.a: This is due to unequal cytokinesis (cell division) during meiosis in oogenesis. The egg needs a large volume of cytoplasm containing nutrients to support the embryo in its first few days after fertilisation, before implantation. The polar bodies are small cells that are essentially just discarded nuclei. Sperm, in contrast, are small and motile, and their main job is to deliver DNA, so they are produced in vast numbers to maximise the chance of one reaching the egg.

faq.q: What would happen if the corpus luteum was not 'rescued' by hCG?
faq.a: If the corpus luteum was not maintained by hCG, it would degenerate around 10-12 days after ovulation, as it normally does in a non-pregnant cycle. This would cause a sharp drop in progesterone and oestrogen levels. The drop in progesterone would lead to the breakdown and shedding of the endometrium (menstruation), resulting in the loss of the early embryo. This is essentially a very early miscarriage.

faq.q: Can substances other than nutrients and waste cross the placenta?
faq.a: Yes. The placenta is permeable to many substances. Unfortunately, this includes harmful substances like alcohol, nicotine, and other drugs, as well as viruses like rubella and HIV. This is why pregnant women are advised to avoid these things. On a positive note, beneficial maternal antibodies (IgG) can also cross the placenta, providing the foetus and newborn with passive immunity for the first few months of life.

sections.content: Human reproduction is a testament to biological precision. It involves a complex interplay of the nervous and endocrine systems to ensure the successful creation, development, and birth of a new individual. In this topic, we will dissect the key hormonal and physiological events that make this process possible.
Gametogenesis: Crafting the Cells of Life

Reproduction begins with the formation of haploid gametes through meiosis. In males, this is called **spermatogenesis**. It occurs continuously in the seminiferous tubules of the testes from puberty onwards. Diploid spermatogonia undergo mitosis and then meiosis to produce four haploid spermatids, which mature into spermatozoa (sperm). This process is stimulated by FSH and testosterone, which is produced under the influence of LH. In females, **oogenesis** occurs in the ovaries. It begins before birth, where oogonia form primary oocytes that are arrested in prophase I. After puberty, each month, one primary oocyte completes meiosis I to form a secondary oocyte and a polar body. The secondary oocyte is arrested in metaphase II and is only completed if fertilisation occurs. This unequal division ensures the ovum is large and nutrient-rich.

items: Spermatogenesis produces four small, motile sperm from one primary spermatocyte.
Oogenesis produces one large, non-motile ovum and 2-3 small polar bodies from one primary oocyte.
Male gamete production is continuous; female production is a monthly cycle with long pauses.

sections.content: The Menstrual Cycle: A Monthly Rhythm

The menstrual cycle is a recurring series of changes in the ovaries and uterus, typically lasting around 28 days, controlled by four key hormones. The cycle begins with the **follicular phase**. The pituitary gland secretes FSH, which stimulates the growth of several ovarian follicles. The developing follicles produce oestrogen, which causes the uterine lining (endometrium) to thicken and repair. As oestrogen levels rise, they initially inhibit FSH (negative feedback) but then, at a peak, stimulate a surge in LH (positive feedback).

The LH surge around day 14 triggers ovulation – the release of the secondary oocyte from the most mature follicle. This marks the start of the **luteal phase**. The remnant of the follicle develops into the corpus luteum, which secretes high levels of progesterone and some oestrogen. Progesterone further prepares the endometrium for implantation and inhibits the pituitary from releasing FSH and LH. If fertilisation does not occur, the corpus luteum degenerates, progesterone levels fall, the endometrium breaks down (menstruation), and the cycle restarts as FSH is no longer inhibited.

sections.question: A blood test on a woman on day 21 of a regular 28-day menstrual cycle shows high levels of progesterone and low levels of FSH and LH. Explain these hormonal levels with reference to the events of the cycle.
sections.solution:
1. Day 21 is in the luteal phase, which occurs after ovulation (around day 14). [1 mark]
2. After ovulation, the corpus luteum forms from the remnant follicle. The corpus luteum secretes high levels of progesterone. [1 mark]
3. The high levels of progesterone (and oestrogen) inhibit the secretion of FSH and LH from the pituitary gland. [1 mark]
4. This inhibition prevents the development of new follicles and another ovulation event during the luteal phase. [1 mark]

sections.content: If intercourse occurs, sperm travel to the oviduct, where fertilisation may take place. The fertilised egg, now a zygote, undergoes several mitotic divisions (cleavage) as it travels towards the uterus, forming a blastocyst. Around 7-8 days after fertilisation, the blastocyst embeds itself into the thickened endometrium in a process called implantation. Upon implantation, the developing embryo starts to secrete **human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG)**. This hormone is crucial as it 'rescues' the corpus luteum, stimulating it to continue producing progesterone and oestrogen. This maintains the endometrium and prevents menstruation, thus securing the pregnancy.

The **placenta** is a remarkable temporary organ that develops from embryonic and maternal tissues. It forms an intimate connection between mother and foetus, but keeps their blood supplies separate. Its large surface area, provided by chorionic villi, and thin barrier facilitate efficient exchange of substances by diffusion. Nutrients, oxygen, and antibodies pass from mother to foetus, while metabolic wastes like carbon dioxide and urea pass from foetus to mother. The placenta also takes over the production of progesterone and oestrogen from the corpus luteum by around 8-10 weeks, maintaining the pregnancy.

sections.question:
The concentration of urea in maternal blood arriving at the placenta is 4.5 mmol dm⁻³. Blood leaving the foetus in the umbilical artery has a urea concentration of 6.2 mmol dm⁻³. Blood returning to the foetus in the umbilical vein has a urea concentration of 4.8 mmol dm⁻³. 
(a) State the direction of net diffusion of urea at the placenta. 
(b) Explain your answer using the data provided.
sections.solution:
(a) Urea diffuses from the foetus to the mother. [1 mark]

(b) The concentration of urea in the umbilical artery (blood from foetus) is 6.2 mmol dm⁻³, which is higher than the concentration in maternal blood (4.5 mmol dm⁻³). [1 mark]
Substances diffuse down a concentration gradient, from a high concentration to a low concentration. [1 mark]
Therefore, urea moves from the foetal blood into the maternal blood across the placenta to be excreted by the mother's kidneys. The lower concentration in the umbilical vein (4.8 mmol dm⁻³) confirms this removal. [1 mark]

sections.content: Birth (parturition) is initiated by hormonal changes. As the pregnancy reaches term, progesterone levels decline, removing their inhibitory effect on uterine contractions. The stretching of the uterus by the foetus triggers the release of **oxytocin** from the posterior pituitary. Oxytocin stimulates the muscular wall of the uterus (myometrium) to contract. These contractions push the baby's head against the cervix, which stimulates nerve impulses that lead to more oxytocin release. This is a classic example of a **positive feedback loop**, where the output (contractions) enhances the original stimulus, leading to increasingly powerful contractions until the baby is delivered.

Be very clear on the difference between positive and negative feedback. Negative feedback (e.g., progesterone inhibiting FSH/LH) maintains homeostasis. Positive feedback (e.g., oxytocin in childbirth) amplifies a change to drive a process to completion. You must be able to provide a biological example for each.
